lens.included_activities: U.S. federal, state and local government establishments judicially administering probation and parole offices and boards and pardon boards, including investigation, risk and needs assessment, supervision planning, case contacts, monitoring, court reporting, violation response, referrals, records and administration. These government operations are analyzed only to characterize AI exposure and service durability.
lens.excluded_activities: Private monitoring, software, treatment, halfway-house and case-management vendors; correctional institutions whose probation or parole function is embedded in a central office; courts, police, prosecutors and public defenders; pretrial-only agencies outside the classification; prisons and jails; and any purported private acquisition target under this government-only NAICS code.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Courts, correctional systems and the public authorize and fund these offices through government budgets rather than commercial revenue. Officers supervise people subject to court or parole conditions and provide investigations and recommendations to judicial or executive decision makers. Workload recurs with active caseloads, entries, exits, contacts, monitoring events and statutory reporting, but there is no private revenue model.
lens.deviation_from_default: The default private LMM acquisition lens cannot apply: the official NAICS definition expressly consists of government establishments and the frozen inputs confirm there is no receipts concept or defensible firm count. The government operating lens is retained only to estimate technological exposure and public-service durability; eligibility and saleability are zero.

a.bridge: Source population: federal probation and pretrial services use cloud case management, mobile information, location and computer monitoring, drug-testing technology and actuarial risk assessment, while a DOJ-funded state project developed dynamic predictive algorithms for rearrest and revocation. Transformation: those demonstrated functions are mapped to case intake, document review and drafting, scheduling, routine contacts, risk triage, alerts, reporting, data entry and resource allocation across U.S. probation and parole offices; field visits, counseling, investigation, crisis response, discretionary recommendations and coercive decisions remain human. Direction of error: the federal system and a funded state project likely have better data and technology than many local offices and bias exposure upward, while rapid generative-document and communications tools may make current examples understate future administrative automation.
a.rationale: A large share of officer and support work involves information gathering, documentation, scheduling, monitoring review, standardized assessment and communication that AI can assist. The work also requires professional judgment, relationship building, procedural fairness, court testimony, safety-sensitive field activity and legally consequential decisions, which bound automation below a majority of the full role in the low and base cases.
a.change_evidence: Representative task-level time studies across federal, state and local offices, paired with measured effects of AI documentation, risk triage, remote contacts and alert management, would materially tighten the range.
caveats: Pretrial services overlap operationally but are not always classified in 922150.
Risk tools supplement rather than replace professional judgment.
Automation of a task does not authorize automated liberty-depriving decisions.

rho.bridge: Source population: federal offices already use actuarial assessment, mobile case data, monitoring technologies and a new cloud case-management system, while DOJ reports practical monitoring tools and a completed dynamic-risk research project. Transformation: five-year realization is discounted for fragmented state and county systems, procurement cycles, legacy data, cybersecurity, model validation, workforce rules, due process, bias and the need for human review. Direction of error: mature federal deployment may overstate local readiness, but resource constraints and high caseloads can accelerate adoption of low-cost documentation and communications tools.
rho.rationale: Existing digital workflows make incremental AI adoption plausible, especially for transcription, summarization, forms, scheduling, alerts and workload prioritization. Realization remains moderate because agencies vary widely, justice data are sensitive, models require validation and explainability, new monitoring can add alerts and workload, and officers remain accountable for recommendations and interventions.
rho.change_evidence: Agency adoption rates, procurement and integration timelines, model audits, officer time saved, alert volumes, false positives, grievance outcomes and implementation costs would materially change realization.
caveats: New technology can increase rather than reduce workload by generating more alerts.
Local agencies may lack data engineering, procurement capacity and cybersecurity resources.
Legal and ethical constraints vary by jurisdiction.

e.rationale: The official Census definition states that this industry consists of government establishments judicially administering probation and parole offices and boards and pardon boards. Government offices are not private LMM firms, and the frozen dataset has neither commercial receipts nor a defensible firm count. Private technology or service contractors belong to other industries and are outside this record.
e.change_evidence: Only a change in the mandated investment universe or NAICS scope to include private contractors could move eligibility above zero; diligence on current 922150 establishments would not.
caveats: Governments may outsource components, but vendors are not 922150 government establishments.
A private-public partnership opportunity would require analysis under the vendor's own industry code.

s5.rationale: A probation or parole office classified in 922150 is a governmental unit exercising public authority, not a privately owned company that can be acquired and resold. Contracts for software, electronic monitoring, treatment or managed services can change hands, but those suppliers are outside this industry and do not make the public office saleable.
s5.change_evidence: No ordinary transaction evidence can change this value under the current private-company acquisition mandate; only a revised scope that explicitly targets concession or vendor contracts would do so.
caveats: This is saleability of the establishment, not procurement opportunity around it.
Government reorganizations or interagency transfers are not M&A exits.

q.rationale: There is no private EBITDA to retain. A public office may retain productivity as greater caseload capacity, faster reports, better prioritization or budget avoidance, but appropriations, headcount rules, oversight and public-service obligations distribute benefits to courts, taxpayers and supervised people. Technology vendors may capture fees, and new monitoring can create additional response workload.
q.change_evidence: Agency budgets, staffing responses, appropriations treatment, vendor fees, caseload changes and service outcomes following deployment would materially change the public-capacity retention estimate, though not create private value capture.
caveats: This is public operational retention, not investor value capture.
Efficiency may be reinvested in service intensity rather than reduce spending.
Poorly targeted monitoring can shift costs to officers and supervised people.

d5.bridge: Source population: BJS counted a large year-end 2024 adult community-supervision population, with a slight one-year decline and a substantial decline over the preceding decade. Transformation: those caseload trends are mapped to five-year demand for probation and parole administration, adjusted for the continuing statutory role, entries and exits, policy shifts between incarceration and community supervision, sentencing reform and supervision intensity. Direction of error: person counts understate work when cases become higher risk or monitoring intensive and overstate it when supervision is shortened, automated or administratively light.
d5.rationale: Millions of adults remain under probation or parole and judicial systems require investigation, supervision and violation processes. The long-term caseload decline argues against growth in the base case, but policy can shift people between incarceration, diversion and community supervision, while risk and service intensity can raise workload even if headcount falls.
d5.change_evidence: Jurisdiction-level sentencing policy, supervision terms, entries, exits, officer caseloads, violation practices, diversion, incarceration substitution and supervision-intensity forecasts would narrow the range.
caveats: National caseload trends conceal large jurisdiction differences.
Demand for staff time is not proportional to the number supervised.
Legislative and court policy can change quickly.

o.rationale: Judicial administration of probation and parole is a statutory government function that cannot be displaced wholesale by a private digital substitute. Outside displacement can occur through incarceration, unconditional release, diversion, shorter terms, consolidation into correctional agencies, private monitoring or treatment contracts and self-service technology, but public officers and boards retain authority and accountability.
o.change_evidence: Legislation, court decisions, privatization rules, supervision-duration reforms, diversion rates and agency-consolidation plans would materially refine outside displacement.
caveats: Technology can change delivery without replacing the public authority.
Incarceration is a policy substitute but not necessarily a lower-cost or desirable one.
Some functions are embedded in central corrections offices and classified elsewhere.

narrative.executive_view: This record is categorically outside a private LMM acquisition universe: NAICS 922150 is composed of government establishments, with no commercial receipts or defensible firm count. Operationally, probation and parole are information-heavy and already use risk tools, monitoring and case systems, so AI can materially assist documentation, triage, scheduling, communications and resource allocation. Human judgment, relationship work, field safety, due process and public authority sharply constrain replacement. The result is a public-sector modernization opportunity, not an acquisition thesis.
narrative.ai_impact: Generative systems can summarize records and contacts, draft routine reports, schedule work, translate communications and surface exceptions. Predictive tools can update risk and prioritize attention; monitoring devices and smartphone workflows can reduce some in-person checks. Officers must still investigate, validate, counsel, respond, testify and make accountable recommendations. Because model improvements may be modest relative to interpretable statistical tools, implementation quality matters more than novelty.
narrative.value_capture: Benefits appear as caseload capacity, timeliness, consistency, safety and potentially better outcomes rather than EBITDA. Agencies may reinvest saved time in higher service intensity, while appropriators, courts, taxpayers, supervised people and vendors share the benefit. Monitoring can add alerts and compliance burdens, so gross labor exposure should not be confused with net budget savings.
narrative.firm_availability: There are no eligible firms or saleable offices under this code. Private suppliers of software, monitoring, treatment, testing and managed services may be investable under other NAICS codes, but including them here would violate the official government-establishment scope and the frozen dataset basis.
narrative.demand_durability: Community supervision remains a large and legally embedded component of the justice system, though the adult caseload has declined materially over a decade. Five-year demand is likely to contract modestly in the base case, with workload affected by supervision intensity, risk mix, policy and violations as much as total headcount.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key operational risks are due process, bias, explainability, privacy, cybersecurity, model drift, inaccurate data, alert overload, unequal technology access, procurement fragmentation and automation deference in liberty-sensitive decisions. For investment scoring, the decisive issue is not uncertainty but categorical ineligibility: l and n are missing because this is government public administration without a receipts concept.
narrative.principal_driver: Large information workloads, established digital case systems and proven risk and monitoring tools create substantial potential for officer augmentation and public-service capacity gains.
narrative.principal_weakness: The industry contains government offices rather than privately acquirable firms, so eligibility and five-year saleability are zero regardless of operational AI potential.

sources.title: NAICS 922150 Parole Offices and Probation Offices
sources.supports: Official definition establishing that the category consists of government establishments judicially administering probation, parole and pardon activities.

sources.title: Probation and Parole in the United States, 2024
sources.supports: Official national caseload, entry and exit context and evidence of recent and decade-long community-supervision trends.

sources.title: Pretrial Risk Assessment
sources.supports: Official evidence for actuarial risk tools used alongside thorough investigation and professional judgment.

sources.title: Monitoring Technologies for Community Supervision
sources.supports: DOJ evidence on location, drug and alcohol monitoring and smartphone applications, plus implementation, equity, workload and resource constraints.

sources.title: AI R&D to Support Community Supervision: Integrated Dynamic Risk Assessment
sources.supports: DOJ-funded evidence for dynamic predictive models and the limited incremental accuracy of some machine-learning methods relative to interpretable statistical models.

sources.title: A Century of Service: Probation and Pretrial Services' Impact on Justice
sources.supports: Official evidence for cloud case management, mobile case information, location and computer monitoring, drug testing and continuing staff responsibility.
